section.title: The Olivet Discourse: Judgment Parables
section.sectionRationale: The discourse ends with three parables of the coming judgment. The ten virgins call for readiness that cannot be borrowed at the last minute; the talents call for faithful, fruitful stewardship until the Master returns; and the sheep and goats reveal the final separation of all nations before the enthroned Son of Man.
section.thesis: Because the Son of Man will surely come to judge, His people must be ready with a readiness no one else can supply, must invest what He entrusts rather than bury it in fear, and must show the reality of their faith by mercy to the least, for how they treat His brothers they treat Him.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the parable of the ten virgins; the parable of the talents; and the judgment of the sheep and the goats.

verses.text: “At that time the kingdom of heaven will be like ten virgins who took their lamps and went out to meet the bridegroom.
Five of them were foolish, and five were wise.
The foolish ones took their lamps but did not take along any extra oil.
But the wise ones took oil in flasks along with their lamps.
When the bridegroom was delayed, they all became drowsy and fell asleep.
At midnight the cry rang out: ‘Here is the bridegroom! Come out to meet him!’
Then all the virgins woke up and trimmed their lamps.
The foolish ones said to the wise, ‘Give us some of your oil; our lamps are going out.’
‘No,’ said the wise ones, ‘or there may not be enough for both us and you. Instead, go to those who sell oil and buy some for yourselves.’
But while they were on their way to buy it, the bridegroom arrived. Those who were ready went in with him to the wedding banquet, and the door was shut.
Later the other virgins arrived and said, ‘Lord, lord, open the door for us!’
But he replied, ‘Truly I tell you, I do not know you.’
Therefore keep watch, because you do not know the day or the hour.
For it is just like a man going on a journey, who called his servants and entrusted them with his possessions.
To one he gave five {{talanton|talents}}, to another two talents, and to another one talent, each according to his own ability. And he went on his journey.
The servant who had received the five talents went at once and put them to work and gained five more.
Likewise, the one with the two talents gained two more.
But the servant who had received the one talent went off, dug a hole in the ground, and hid his master’s money.
After a long time the master of those servants returned and settled accounts with them.
The servant who had received the five talents came and presented five more. ‘Master,’ he said, ‘you entrusted me with five talents. See, I have gained five more.’
His master replied, ‘Well done, good and faithful servant! You have been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things. Enter into the joy of your master!’
The servant who had received the two talents also came and said, ‘Master, you entrusted me with two talents. See, I have gained two more.’
His master replied, ‘Well done, good and faithful servant! You have been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things. Enter into the joy of your master!’
Finally, the servant who had received the one talent came and said, ‘Master, I knew that you are a hard man, reaping where you have not sown and gathering where you have not scattered seed.
So I was afraid and went out and hid your talent in the ground. See, you have what belongs to you.’
‘You wicked, lazy servant!’ replied his master. ‘You knew that I reap where I have not sown and gather where I have not scattered seed.
Then you should have deposited my money with the bankers, and on my return I would have received it back with interest.
Therefore take the talent from him and give it to the one who has ten talents.
For everyone who has will be given more, and he will have an abundance. But the one who does not have, even what he has will be taken away from him.
And throw that worthless servant into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.’

units.label: The sheep and the goats
units.note: as you did to the least, you did to Me

verses.text: When the Son of Man comes in His glory, and all the angels with Him, He will sit on His glorious throne.
All the nations will be gathered before Him, and He will separate the people one from another, as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats.
He will place the sheep on His right and the goats on His left.
Then the King will say to those on His right, ‘Come, you who are blessed by My Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world.
For I was hungry and you gave Me something to eat, I was thirsty and you gave Me something to drink, I was a stranger and you took Me in,
I was naked and you clothed Me, I was sick and you looked after Me, I was in prison and you visited Me.’
Then the righteous will answer Him, ‘Lord, when did we see You hungry and feed You, or thirsty and give You something to drink?
When did we see You a stranger and take You in, or naked and clothe You?
When did we see You sick or in prison and visit You?’
And the King will reply, ‘Truly I tell you, whatever you did for one of the {{elachistos|least}} of these brothers of Mine, you did for Me.’
Then He will say to those on His left, ‘Depart from Me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.
For I was hungry and you gave Me nothing to eat, I was thirsty and you gave Me nothing to drink,
I was a stranger and you did not take Me in, I was naked and you did not clothe Me, I was sick and in prison and you did not look after Me.’
And they too will reply, ‘Lord, when did we see You hungry or thirsty or a stranger or naked or sick or in prison, and did not minister to You?’
Then the King will answer, ‘Truly I tell you, whatever you did not do for one of the least of these, you did not do for Me.’
And they will go away into {{aionios|eternal}} punishment, but the righteous into eternal life.”

talanton.gloss: A talent, a very large sum of money, about twenty years' wages for a laborer. In the parable it stands for whatever the Master entrusts to His servants, gifts, resources, opportunities, the gospel itself, to be invested for Him, not buried in fear.

elachistos.gloss: Least, smallest, most insignificant. The King identifies Himself with the least of these, the hungry, the stranger, the sick, the imprisoned; how one treats them reveals the heart's true response to Him, for in serving them one serves Him.

aionios.gloss: Eternal, everlasting, belonging to the age to come. The same word describes both the punishment of the cursed and the life of the righteous; the two destinies are of equal, unending duration, and the judgment is final.

basic.context: Jesus closes the discourse with three parables about His return and judgment. Ten virgins wait for a bridegroom; five bring extra oil and are ready, five do not and are shut out. A master entrusts talents to three servants; two invest and are rewarded, one buries his in fear and is condemned. Finally the Son of Man gathers all nations and separates sheep from goats, welcoming those who served Him in the hungry, the stranger, the sick, and the prisoner, and rejecting those who did not, for as they treated the least, they treated Him.
basic.hermeneutics: The three parables build on the call to watch: readiness, faithfulness, and mercy. The oil pictures a real preparedness that cannot be borrowed or faked at the last moment. The talents warn against fearful passivity; faith works. The sheep and goats show that genuine faith bears the fruit of love; the deeds do not earn salvation but reveal it.
basic.originalLanguages: The master entrusts talanta, talents, huge sums, to be invested. The King identifies with the elachistoi, the least, so that serving them is serving Him. And the two destinies are both aionios, eternal, unending life or unending punishment.
basic.typology: The bridegroom and the wedding banquet fulfill the prophetic image of God as husband and the messianic marriage supper. The Son of Man enthroned to judge the nations fulfills Daniel 7. The Shepherd separating sheep from goats echoes Ezekiel 34.
basic.christ: Jesus is the delayed but certain Bridegroom, the returning Master who settles accounts, and the enthroned Son of Man and King who judges all nations. He is the King so united with His people that kindness to the least is kindness to Him.
basic.god: God prepared the kingdom for the blessed from the foundation of the world and the eternal fire for the devil and his angels. He rewards faithful stewardship, welcomes mercy done to the least, and justly judges fruitless, loveless unbelief.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 pictures God separating the sheep. Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man enthroned. Luke 19 parallels the parable of the pounds. James 2 teaches that faith without works is dead.
basic.oneStory: The last discourse ends by fixing the disciples' eyes on the judgment that closes history: the Bridegroom comes, the Master returns, the King is enthroned. Between now and then, His people are to be ready, faithful, and merciful. The story of the kingdom will reach its consummation when the Son of Man separates all humanity, welcoming His own into the joy of their Master and the kingdom prepared from the foundation of the world.

context.literary: These three parables complete the Olivet Discourse, applying its call to watch. They escalate: the virgins stress being ready, the talents stress being faithful, and the sheep and goats reveal the criterion of judgment and the final destinies. The sheep and goats scene, with the enthroned Son of Man, forms a majestic climax to all Jesus' teaching before the passion narrative begins.
context.historical: A Jewish wedding involved the bridegroom coming, often after delay, to take his bride to the feast; attendants needed lamps for the night procession. A talent was an enormous sum, so even the one-talent servant was trusted with much. Shepherds in Palestine pastured sheep and goats together but separated them at times, the goats needing more shelter at night; the imagery of right and left denoted favor and disfavor.
context.canonical: The separation of sheep and goats fulfills Ezekiel 34:17-22, where God judges between the sheep. The enthroned Son of Man judging the nations draws on Daniel 7:13-14 and Joel 3. The identification of the King with the needy echoes Proverbs 19:17, kindness to the poor lends to the LORD, and coheres with James 2 and 1 John 3 on faith proven by love.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press home the certainty of the Son of Man's return and judgment, calling disciples to a readiness that cannot be borrowed, a faithfulness that invests what God entrusts, and a mercy to the least that reveals true, saving faith.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables describe the coming judgment and its outcomes, and they prescribe: be ready, be faithful, be merciful. The sheep and goats show that deeds of love are the evidence, not the earning, of belonging to Christ, warning against a faith that produces nothing.

debates.question: Who are the least of these brothers of Mine in the judgment of the nations?

views.label: Christ's disciples and gospel messengers
views.case: Brothers of Mine elsewhere in Matthew means Jesus' followers, and the least of these may be His persecuted disciples and missionaries; the nations are judged by how they received Christ's representatives and thus Christ Himself.

views.label: All who are needy and suffering
views.case: The list of hunger, thirst, sickness, and imprisonment reads as universal human need, so the King identifies with the poor and afflicted generally; genuine faith shows itself in mercy to any of the least, in whom Christ is met.

debates.bearing: Both agree that how one treats the vulnerable reveals one's true response to Christ; whether the least are His disciples or the needy at large, mercy to them is mercy to the King, and its absence exposes unbelief.

study.originalLanguages: The master distributes talanta, talents, immense sums, to be put to work, and condemns the servant who, gripped by fear, buries his in the ground; faith is meant to invest, not to hide. In the final scene the King astonishes both the righteous and the cursed by identifying Himself with the elachistoi, the least, so that food for the hungry and welcome for the stranger were given to Him, and their neglect was neglect of Him. The verdict is weighty and final: the cursed go into aionios punishment and the righteous into aionios life, the same word marking both, two everlasting destinies decided by the King on His glorious throne.

typology.type: The bridegroom and the wedding feast
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Bridegroom coming for His bride, the church, to the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:6-9.

typology.type: The shepherd separating sheep and goats
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man judging between His flock, fulfilling Ezekiel 34:17-22.

typology.type: The enthroned Son of Man judging the nations
typology.fulfillment: Daniel 7:13-14, the one given dominion, now the King on His glorious throne who judges all peoples.

study.christ: Jesus is the Bridegroom whose coming, though delayed, is certain, and who welcomes the ready into the feast while the unready find the door shut. He is the Master who entrusts His servants with much and returns to settle accounts, rewarding the faithful with His own joy. And He is the Son of Man who comes in glory to sit on His throne and judge all nations, the King so identified with His people that every cup of water given to the least is given to Him. He is both the criterion and the reward of the judgment: to know and love Him is to serve the least, and to enter, at last, the kingdom prepared from the foundation of the world.
study.god: God is the King who has prepared a kingdom for the blessed from the foundation of the world, and, soberingly, an eternal fire for the devil and his angels, into which the unrepentant share his fate. He is generous, entrusting real treasure to His servants and delighting to say well done and to share His joy. He sees the heart through its deeds, welcoming the mercy shown to the least as shown to Himself and exposing the loveless neglect that unmasks unbelief. He is the just Judge who assigns two final, everlasting destinies, and the gracious Father who calls His children to inherit the kingdom.

crossReferences.note: God judging between the sheep and the goats, behind the final separation.
The Son of Man enthroned with dominion over all peoples, the Judge of the nations.
Faith without works is dead, illuminating the deeds that reveal true faith.
Kindness to the poor is lending to the LORD, echoed in serving the King in the least.

study.oneStory: The final discourse closes by fixing the disciples' eyes on the end of the story: the Bridegroom comes, the Master returns, the King is enthroned to judge all nations. Between now and then, His people live ready, faithful, and merciful, their love for the least revealing their love for Him. History will reach its consummation when the Son of Man separates all humanity and welcomes His own into the kingdom prepared from the foundation of the world. The next chapter turns to the cross, where the King and Judge first gives His own life to open that kingdom to sinners.

quiz.q: What is the main lesson of the parable of the ten virgins?

options.text: Be ready for the Bridegroom's coming with a preparedness that cannot be borrowed at the last moment.
options.note: Verses 10-13 stress readiness that the foolish could not obtain once it was too late.

options.text: Do not fall asleep while waiting for the Lord.
options.note: All ten slept; the difference was the oil, that is, real readiness, not staying awake as such.

options.text: Always share your resources with those who have none.
options.note: The wise could not share the oil; the point is personal readiness, not sharing.

options.text: The Bridegroom will come exactly when expected.
options.note: He was delayed and came at an unexpected hour, hence the call to be ready.

quiz.q: Why was the one-talent servant condemned?

options.text: He buried his master's talent in fear instead of putting it to work.
options.note: Verses 24-27 show his fearful passivity as the fault, not any loss incurred.

options.text: He lost the talent his master had given him.
options.note: He did not lose it; he returned it intact, but he failed to invest it, which was the fault.

options.text: He was given less than the other servants.
options.note: The amount was not the issue; the two-talent servant, with less than the first, was commended.

options.text: He tried to invest but failed to make a profit.
options.note: He did not try at all; he hid the talent in the ground.

quiz.q: On what basis does the King welcome the sheep in the final judgment?

options.text: Their mercy to the least, which revealed their true response to the King Himself.
options.note: Verses 34-40 show deeds of mercy to the least as evidence of belonging to Christ.

options.text: The good deeds by which they earned their place in the kingdom.
options.note: The deeds reveal, rather than earn, salvation; the kingdom was prepared for them from the foundation of the world.

options.text: Their careful avoidance of doing anything wrong.
options.note: The judgment turns on active mercy shown, not merely on avoiding wrong.

options.text: Their success and prosperity in life.
options.note: The criterion is mercy to the needy, not personal success.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about the two final destinies?

options.text: The cursed go into eternal punishment and the righteous into eternal life, both everlasting and final.
options.note: Verse 46 sets the two eternal outcomes side by side with the same word.

options.text: The righteous receive eternal life, and the rest simply cease to exist.
options.note: The text names eternal punishment, using the same word as eternal life, not annihilation.

options.text: Everyone is eventually welcomed into the kingdom.
options.note: Jesus clearly separates two groups with two destinies, not universal welcome.

options.text: The destinies are temporary and can later be reversed.
options.note: The judgment is final; both outcomes are described as eternal.

questions: The foolish virgins could not borrow oil at the last minute. What kind of readiness for Christ can only be built now, not scrambled for later?
The fearful servant buried his talent rather than risk it. Where is fear keeping you from investing what God has entrusted to you?
The King is met in the hungry, the stranger, the sick, and the prisoner. Whose need in front of you might actually be an encounter with Christ?
The righteous were surprised they had served the King at all. What would it mean to serve the least without keeping score, simply out of love?
Two eternal destinies hang on this judgment. How does the reality of Christ's return and judgment reshape what you give your life to this week?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts add when the Son of Man comes; the command keep watch, because you do not know the day or the hour restates the discourse's central charge.

The source separates the amounts from the clause each according to his own ability with a dash; it is rendered here with a comma for a clean, dash-free reading. A talent was about twenty years' wages.
The same Greek word, aionios, describes both eternal punishment and eternal life, so the two destinies are presented as equally lasting and final.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
